raw_response:
I fully agree with the conclusion reached earlier. The fact that all affected females consistently transmit the trait while affected males do not affect their daughters supports this being X-linked dominant inheritance. In such cases, since affected males typically have another normal allele from their mother, they often appear unaffected; however, they still risk passing along the disease-causing allele to any son who receives the Y-chromosome from him. Furthermore, because females have two X chromosomes, those who are carriers (heterozygotes) will almost certainly pass one problematic X onto half of their progeny. Therefore, option C remains the best fit based on these genetic patterns.

Final answer: **C**.
